learning_outcomes.description: Describe the foundation principles and regulatory frameworks that guide and govern professional practice in computing, including relevant codes of conduct and Australian laws pertaining to technology.
Apply ethical decision-making frameworks to anticipate the social, environmental and ethical impact of technological products and development processes.
Explain legal & ethical responsibilities associated with issues related to privacy, security, diversity, inclusion and systems reliability in computing practice.
Identify and critically reflect on personal biases and their potential influence on technological design, development and deployment.

assessments.task_description:
Throughout the semester, individual or group tasks will be assigned. Students are to document their completion of the tasks each week, and to compile those tasks into an individual portfolio submission. An interim opportunity for formative feedback on your completed tasks will be available at the end of Week 5 (i.e. before the Census Date).

Information sources (including AI Tools)

You may use Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools (including Generative AI and Machine Translation tools), and other external sources or systems to support this assessment. Their use is optional, and choosing not to use AI tools will not disadvantage you.

You remain responsible for determining what knowledge and assistance are appropriate, evaluating the relevance and limitations of contributions, verifying consequential claims, and deciding what should influence your final system map and analysis. Any use of AI tools must be acknowledged in accordance with the course requirements. Your Accountable Knowledge Integration Statement must explain the role of any consequential human or technological contributions and how you evaluated and integrated them. Failure to acknowledge AI tools use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
Proposal (non-graded formative) 21/08/2026 5:00 pm

Case Study Report (graded with Interview) 16/10/2026 5:00 pm

Oral Interview (graded with Report) 27/10/2026 5:00 pm

This is a sequence comprising three assessments: a non-graded Proposal to confirm the appropriateness of your case study; a Case Study Report and an Oral Interview (both combined in grading).
assessments.task_description:
Analyse a computing technology case with respect to the ethical topics covered in this course. Your case study will apply ethical frameworks and principles to a sociotechnical system, evaluating it with respect to, e.g. professional codes of ethics, bias and fairness, accessibility, consent, transparency, privacy, security, relevant legal frameworks and governance.

This is a sequence comprising three assessments: a non-graded proposal to confirm the appropriateness of your case study, a case study report and an oral interview.

The proposal is a non-graded formative submission that can provide feedback to students before the census date. Students will get feedback on their proposal and learn whether it is appropriate for the course as is, or would be better to revise.

Your grade for this assessment is based on your case study report and the oral interview combined. These are not graded separately and your oral interview forms the secure component in this assessment. To be clear, students need to pass (satisfactory) the interview otherwise their grade for this assessment item will be capped at a 3. Details will be provided as part of the assessment brief.

In accordance with UQ Assessment Policy, your oral interview will be recorded.

Information sources (including AI Tools)

You may use Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools (including Generative AI and Machine Translation tools), and other external sources or systems to support this assessment. Their use is optional, and choosing not to use AI tools will not disadvantage you.

You remain responsible for determining what knowledge and assistance are appropriate, evaluating the relevance and limitations of contributions, verifying consequential claims, and deciding what should influence your final system map and analysis. Any use of AI tools must be acknowledged in accordance with the course requirements. Your Accountable Knowledge Integration Statement must explain the role of any consequential human or technological contributions and how you evaluated and integrated them. Failure to acknowledge AI tools use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.

assessments.task_description:
The final exam (including any deferred or supplementary exams) are closed-book examinations and no calculators or student notes of any kind are permitted in the examination room.

As the final exam (including any deferred or supplementary exams) are in-person technology-free paper-based secure exams, Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are not available to students while performing this assessment.
